# Chapter 14

[3n0risk9] Dawn came thin and mean, the kind that didn’t promise warmth so much as it promised heat later, like a threat delivered politely. The safe house had turned the night’s violence into a smell that clung to everything—blood iron, sweat sour, concrete dust. Flynn stood outside with his back to the wall and watched the horizon lighten. Somewhere inside, Khoury made a small sound now and then, like a dog dreaming of being kicked.

[ji0kvxd1] Cage slept on the floor like he’d earned it. One arm over his eyes, boots still on, mouth slightly open. Birdie sat with his knees pulled up, staring at his hands. He kept rubbing his thumb over a strip of duct tape stuck to his skin. When Flynn looked at him, Birdie tried to smile and couldn’t make the muscles obey.

[ess37lv9] The tablet on the folding table blinked with a map overlay. Khoury’s garage was tagged in green. Three vehicles behind biometric gates, climate-controlled like museum relics, insured for sums Flynn couldn’t hold in his head. Cage had keyed in a route that avoided the arcology’s main roads. He’d highlighted blind spots in the drone coverage like they were scenic overlooks.

[7hbbq3sf] Khoury sat slumped in the chair, zip ties digging into his wrists. The stun baton lay on the table between him and Cage, a quiet object that didn’t need to announce itself. Khoury’s face was swelling where Cage had hit him. One cheekbone purple already, like fruit left too long in sun. His eyes followed the baton the way prey watches a snake.

[ljmkj2xo] “He’s got a family,” Flynn said, and hated that it sounded like bargaining. Cage didn’t move. He opened one eye and looked at Flynn as if Flynn had spoken a foreign language badly. “So do lots of people,” Cage said. “We’re not taking them. We’re taking the cars.” He sat up, joints popping, and smiled like he’d just won something.

[jpii01gw] Birdie whispered, “We said we’d call medical.” The words came out as if he were reminding himself of an oath. Cage stood and stretched, the bruise on his knuckles dark and wet-looking. “We will,” Cage said. “After.” Flynn knew that meant after the thing Cage wanted, not after Khoury needed.

[tenityck] They moved Khoury like cargo. Cage hauled him to his feet by the back of his shirt and steered him out into the morning, toward the van. Khoury stumbled, half blind with fear and exhaustion. Flynn caught his elbow once when he almost fell, and felt the man flinch as if Flynn’s hand were another weapon.

[taviffn6] The drive back into Phoenix was stranger in daylight. At night the city’s wealth looked like a glow, abstract and distant. In the morning it was concrete and glass and water features running on stolen rivers. The streets were full of pods gliding silently in tight lanes, each one sealed, each one with a face behind tinted plastic. Flynn watched them pass and wondered if any of those people would ever have a reason to be afraid.

[hb2xtqrk] Cage sat in the passenger seat with Khoury between him and Birdie in the back, arms pinned, zip ties biting. Cage scrolled through Khoury’s access permissions like he was shopping. “You ever drive these?” Cage asked without looking up. Khoury shook his head once, a jerk of denial, then stopped because movement hurt.

[yw8dy83j] “No,” Khoury rasped. “They’re—preserved.” Cage laughed softly. “Preserved,” he said, tasting it. “Like meat in a jar. Like a world that thinks it can save itself by putting everything in plastic.” He leaned closer. “You kept them sterile so they’d never disappoint you.”

[hdyfaa6p] The neighborhood gates recognized Khoury’s biometrics before they recognized his fear. Cameras tracked their van and decided it belonged, because the system liked paperwork more than it liked truth. The gate slid open. The landscaping inside looked more alive than anything outside the arcology, engineered greens and fake birdsong piped through hidden speakers. Flynn felt like he’d driven into a simulation.

[am3551uz] The garage was a private climate, cool enough to make Flynn’s skin prickle. The air smelled like polished leather and the chemical sweetness of preservation foam. A line of cars sat under translucent covers that glowed softly from the overhead lights, as if each one were lit from within. Even the dust looked curated.

[xlwetfwh] Cage walked among them with reverence that wasn’t respect. He peeled a cover back with two fingers and exposed the Charger’s black paint, mirror-deep. Chrome trim clean as teeth. Flynn saw his own face in it, warped and pale. He expected to feel awe the way he had in the boneyard when they’d first dragged a dead engine into sunlight. Instead he felt a coldness, like the car was a corpse dressed for a funeral.

[rn2u9ogt] “’Sixty-nine,” Cage said. He ran his hand along the fender, leaving a smear of sweat that dried quickly in the conditioned air. “This is what they banned. Not because of emissions. Because of who it made you.” He glanced back at Khoury. “You ever sit in it? Smell it? Or did you just look?”

[ea7dsm46] Khoury’s mouth moved before sound came out. “They’re assets,” he said, voice cracking. “Historical. I—kept them safe.” Cage nodded as if taking that in. Then he said, “Safe. Right.” He turned and yanked the cover off the Chevelle, red as fresh blood, and the color hit Flynn like a warning sign.

[33257m7z] Birdie hovered near the door, hands shaking, eyes darting to the cameras mounted in the ceiling corners. “Time,” Birdie whispered. Cage ignored him. Cage was performing now, and performances didn’t respect clocks. He walked to the Camaro, ‘seventy-three, blue so deep it looked like a bruise. “You picked good,” Cage told Khoury, almost kindly.

[vg812wse] Khoury tried to stand straighter in the chair they’d shoved him into. He looked around his own garage like it had been invaded by ghosts. “You can take them,” he said, bargaining again, the way rich men tried to buy their way back to normal. “Just—don’t hurt my family. Don’t come back.”

[plzbrf13] Cage crouched in front of him. His eyes were bright, attentive, the way they got when he was about to explain a joke. “We’re not coming back,” Cage said. “Not for you. You’re not special. You’re just convenient.” He tapped Khoury’s chest once with two fingers, a mock check for a heartbeat. “You’ll heal. You’ll buy more cars. You’ll tell your friends some story about terrorists and trauma and the system will wrap you in a blanket.”

[9l5dos1s] “You’re destroying my life,” Khoury said, and this time he sounded like he meant it. His voice had a raw edge, not fear but grief, like a man watching his house burn while the fire department checks for permits. Cage shrugged. “Your life’s in your head,” Cage said. “You can download a new one.”

[9ynn46v8] Flynn heard himself speak, surprising even him. “It’s not like that,” he said. Cage’s gaze snapped to him, amused. “It is exactly like that,” Cage said. “That’s the whole point of their miracle. They made pain optional. They made death a service interruption. They turned consequence into a notification you can swipe away.” He smiled again. “We’re swiping the other direction.”

[llzaxl7a] They got the doors open with Khoury’s code and his face held to the scanner like a hostage passport. The garage portals hissed. Beyond them, a private service lane led to a loading area where collectors could receive shipments without letting the city see them. Cage had a dolly, straps, a portable fuel bladder. He’d prepared the way a man prepares for a wedding.

[hjhizbwo] The cars were perfect and useless without fuel. Cage found their emergency preserve tanks—just enough to move them around, not enough to flee. He laughed at that too. “Even the gasoline is rationed,” he said. “They want the shape of danger without the danger.” He hooked the fuel bladder to the Charger and pumped. The smell of real gas hit Flynn hard, sweet and violent, and for a second his body woke up as if it recognized a language older than his own childhood.

[1ywm7r7b] Birdie drove the Charger first, hands trembling on the wheel. The engine turned over with a sound that didn’t belong in this century, a thick throat clearing itself, a low animal rumble that made the pods outside feel like toys. Birdie’s face changed, fear replaced by a grin that looked almost like prayer. Cage watched him with satisfaction, the way a dealer watches a first hit.

[i4qoyfhr] Flynn took the Chevelle. The seat hugged him in a way no pod ever had. The dash was old and honest, gauges meant for eyes not algorithms. When he turned the key, the car shuddered and came alive beneath him. He felt the vibration in his ribs. It should have been beautiful. It was. And it was poisoned.

[x8akka8m] Cage chose the Camaro for himself, of course. He revved it once, loud, and the sound bounced around the cooled garage like gunfire. Khoury flinched. Cage leaned out the window and said, “Hear that? That’s what you bought and never used. That’s what you kept in a box like a child’s toy.” Then he added, softer, almost intimate: “You didn’t deserve it.”

[z83r0ckv] They rolled out in a stagger, three fossils moving under their own power, leaving behind a space that felt suddenly empty and obscene. The service lane spit them into a wider road where the pods flowed like water, silent and orderly. Heads turned. A few people lifted phones. A drone dipped to inspect, its camera eye narrowing, then backed off when it read Khoury’s authorized signature in the system. The world trusted the right metadata.

[828r2mv1] Flynn kept his eyes on the lane markers and tried not to imagine Khoury’s wife waking up in a house full of tape and screams. The Chevelle’s steering was heavy, honest work. Every correction was physical. He could feel the road texture through the wheel, every small fracture. He thought of Diesel saying if you come back, you’re going to be different, and he wondered if different was just another word for ruined.

[a8lmnjiz] They ditched the cars in an abandoned warehouse district at the city’s edge, a place that used to be industrial before everything got automated and moved inside the climate bubble. Concrete hulks. Broken windows. Silence. Cage had picked it because it still had dead corners in the network. They parked the three cars nose to tail like a parade waiting to start.

[25xcfguh] Khoury was left on a dirty office chair, zip-tied, gag removed because Cage wanted to hear him talk. Birdie hovered as if he might cut the ties, but he didn’t. Flynn stood in the doorway, watching dust float in the shafts of light. “You said you’d call,” Birdie said again, voice small. Cage nodded and held up a cheap burner.

[k9udpcps] Cage made the call like a man ordering takeout. He gave an address, said there was a man in need of medical assistance, said he’d been assaulted, said hurry. He didn’t say sorry. He didn’t use Khoury’s name. When he hung up, he looked at Khoury and said, “They’ll patch you up. You’ll get the good drugs. You’ll be fine.”

[pqtb64xv] Khoury stared at him with a hatred so clean it looked like faith. “You’re not racers,” Khoury whispered. “You’re animals.” Cage smiled. “Racing is animal,” Cage said. “That’s why they banned it.” He leaned in close enough that Flynn could smell gasoline on his breath. “You built a world to keep the animals out. We’re just reminding you what you are.”

[8mzub1gl] The run back to the compound was pure desert. They took back roads until the city’s last clean lines fell away and the land opened, brutal and indifferent. The three cars followed the van like bright trophies dragged through dust. When the compound came into view, Diesel was standing in the yard with her arms crossed, a cigarette burning down to her fingers. She looked at the cars first. Then she looked at Flynn’s face. Her eyes hardened like cooling metal.

[frv32eyy] Cage cut the engines one by one, letting the silence fall like a tarp. He climbed out of the Camaro with a grin that belonged on a boy showing off a stolen bottle. “Look what Phoenix keeps in cages,” he said. Diesel didn’t answer. She walked past Cage without looking at him and stopped in front of Flynn. Up close, the grease on her hands smelled like work, like something honest.

[y9y5ymkd] “What happened?” she asked. Not what did you get, not how did you do it. What happened. Flynn tried to open his mouth and found only the taste of pool water and duct tape. He saw Khoury’s kids in the glass wall. He saw the baton at the throat. He shook his head once. Diesel’s jaw tightened. “Don’t lie to me,” she said, but her voice didn’t rise.

[8fqkaaks] Cage came up behind Flynn and put a hand on his shoulder, possessive as a brand. “It went fine,” Cage said. “We got what we needed.” Diesel finally looked at him. Her eyes were flat. “Fine,” she repeated, and the word sounded like a spit. “You bring cops to our door and call it fine.” Cage’s smile didn’t move. “Cops don’t come out here,” he said. “They don’t like the heat.”

[wa9n8001] Birdie climbed out of the Charger last, moving like his bones hurt. He stared at Diesel as if she were the last adult on earth. “Diesel,” he began. Diesel lifted a hand to stop him. “Go inside,” she said. Birdie hesitated, then obeyed. Flynn watched him go and felt something sick in his stomach, like a rope tightening. Cage watched Birdie too, pleased the way a man is pleased when a dog returns.

[5x2tpvfm] In the shop, Diesel didn’t ask again. She just set a kettle on a hot plate and waited for the water to fail to boil in the desert air. Flynn stood in the doorway, hands hanging useless at his sides. Cage prowled around the Charger’s engine bay like a priest around an altar. “We should strip two,” Cage said. “Sell parts. Keep one for Mexico.” Diesel said, “You kidnapped a man,” and the sentence landed heavy, undeniable.

[s1cg8y7v] Cage didn’t flinch. “We borrowed him,” he said. “We returned him.” Diesel stared at him long enough that Flynn thought she might hit him. Instead she looked at Flynn again. “Did you see his face?” she asked softly. Flynn nodded. Diesel closed her eyes for a moment, like she was measuring something in her head and finding it insufficient. “That’s a scar you don’t revive from,” she said.

[404k67xv] Later, when the sun was high enough to make the air shimmer, the compound’s cheap screen lit up with a news feed Diesel hadn’t asked for. A clean-faced anchor in a studio that looked like it had never seen dust spoke over footage of Khoury’s neighborhood: police drones, medics, a stretcher wheeling out a man with a swollen face. The headline read KIDNAPPING ATTEMPT THWARTED, as if the system couldn’t admit it had been beaten.

[ya19aqtu] Cage watched the report with calm interest, like a man watching weather. “They’ll call it domestic terrorism,” he said. “They always do. It makes the pods feel safer.” The anchor mentioned a task force, rewards, biometric sweeps. Flynn felt his implant buzz faintly as if it had heard its own name. Diesel muted the screen and the silence came back, thicker this time. Outside, the three cars sat in the yard, gleaming in the dirt, and for the first time Flynn understood they weren’t trophies. They were bait.
